story:
Eight-year-old Lucy stepped onto the patio in her new yellow sundress, head tilted just so to catch the sun on the diamond barrette she had placed perfectly in her hair. She admired her pink Crocs, a gift from Dad after a trip to a place she couldn’t pronounce. The backyard shone under a bright first-day-of-summer sky. “Florence, look how bright the sun is today!” she said. Florence agreed.

Lucy pointed at the tall old tree. Its bark looked strange, almost alive. Up close she saw why: ants were crawling through the crevices, busy and purposeful. “What are they doing?” she asked. Maybe hauling sap, Florence guessed. A line of ants set off from the trunk. As Lucy followed, she heard old man Barchie’s ancient Ford rattle down the alley and lifted her nose at the smell of exhaust as he turned the corner. The ants marched toward the anthill at the back fence, some carrying tiny pieces of bark. Lucy stood a few feet away. “Stay there. Don’t move,” Florence said. “Maybe they won’t notice us.” Lucy waited, but watching ants turned dull.

Florence suggested flowers instead. By the white fence, Mom’s hydrangeas wore blue hats, daisies twirled in yellow dresses, and the pink carnations crowded together like pompoms. The fence gleamed so brightly that Lucy lifted a hand to shield her eyes. A small green bush stood among the flowers. “Why did Mom plant a green bush? It doesn’t have a smell,” she wondered. “Yes, it does. Get closer,” Florence said. Lucy leaned in. The bush smelled green—resinous and clean, like a pine tree. Each plant had its own smell, different even from the grass.

On impulse, Lucy flopped onto a wide patch of lawn and rolled, pressing her face into different angles of grass until her sundress gathered stains. The scent was wonderful and alive. When she looked up, a cloud in the blue sky shaped itself like a dog sitting and gazing upward. “That’s Bruce. He was my dog. He’s saying hello.” Florence said it was possible; Bruce had loved Lucy. Lucy remembered chasing him around the yard, his jumping paws, the garden hose spraying arcs as he snapped at the water. She laughed at the memory.

She rose and wandered back to the patio, taking in the whole yard: the garage to the right with its door inviting, the hose coiled neat against the house, the sidewalk leading to the garage, the sliding glass door reflecting the bright day. The air smelled warm and golden. She spun with her arms stretched wide, then tucked herself into the red Adirondack chair, knees hugged to her chest, watching the sky until Bruce drifted away.

Mom stepped out with a tray of lemonade and cookies. “There you are! I heard you talking to your friend again.” Lucy nodded. They had talked about Bruce. “Is she happy out here?” Mom asked. “She lives out here most of the time,” Lucy said. Mom smiled. “Is she still on your shoulder?” “Yes, Mom,” Lucy answered. “She’s a fairy.”
